steps.prompt: Which word is the finite verb?

hints.Fili: 'Fili' is a noun meaning 'Son' in the vocative
hints.mei: 'Mei' is a pronoun meaning 'of me' in the genitive

hints.to send: 'To send' is 'mittere' — 'miserere' comes from 'misereri' (to have mercy)
hints.to be miserable: 'To be miserable' is 'miserum esse' — 'misereri' is a deponent meaning 'to pity'

steps.reveal: miserere = have mercy! (2nd sg. imperative of deponent 'misereor')

hints.present indicative: Present indicative would be 'misereris' — 'miserere' is the imperative
hints.infinitive: The infinitive is 'misereri' — 'miserere' is the singular imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'miserearis' — 'miserere' is imperative

hints.mitto, mittere, misi, missum: These are the parts of 'mittere' (to send)
hints.metior, metiri, mensus sum, —: These are the parts of 'metiri' (to measure) — also a deponent
hints.misceo, miscere, miscui, mixtum: These are the parts of 'miscere' (to mix)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'filius' — 'Fili' with -i is the vocative of 2nd declension -ius nouns
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'filii' — 'Fili' is the special vocative form

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'filii' ends in -ii
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'filii' ends in -ii
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'filii' ends in -ii

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'ego' — 'mei' is the genitive of 'ego'
hints.dative: Dative would be 'mihi' — 'misereor' governs the genitive, not dative
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'me' — 'mei' is genitive, required by 'misereor'

steps.target: it is a special deponent verb that governs the genitive of the person pitied
choices: it is a special deponent verb that governs the genitive of the person pitied
it is an ablative that looks like genitive

hints.all deponent verbs take genitive: Most deponent verbs take accusative — 'misereor' is special in taking genitive
hints.it is an ablative that looks like geniti: 'Mei' is distinctly genitive — ablative of 'ego' would be 'me'
hints.the genitive is partitive: Partitive genitive indicates a part of a whole — here 'mei' is the person pitied

translation: Son of David, have mercy on me
